title: Bloody diarrhea flare in an immunosuppressed patient with recent antibiotics

chart.oneLiner: 32F with known ulcerative colitis (on azathioprine), type 1 diabetes, and a recent clindamycin course for a dental abscess presents with 10 days of worsening bloody, mucoid diarrhea up to 12 times daily with urgency, tenesmus, crampy lower abdominal pain, low-grade fever, and lightheadedness.

opening: I have colitis, but this flare is the worst it's ever been — I'm in the bathroom passing blood and mucus a dozen times a day and I feel weak and dizzy.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 32-year-old woman, pale and fatigued, frightened because this flare is far worse than her usual disease. Good historian but tires easily. Lightheaded when she stands.

HPI: Known ulcerative colitis for 4 years, usually well controlled on azathioprine. Over the past 10 days, escalating BLOODY diarrhea with mucus, fecal URGENCY, TENESMUS, and crampy lower abdominal pain — now up to 10–12 stools per day including several times overnight. Low-grade fevers and chills. New LIGHTHEADEDNESS and palpitations when standing, and muscle weakness/cramps in her legs. Poor oral intake for days. About 3 weeks ago she finished a 7-day course of CLINDAMYCIN for a dental abscess (reveal only if asked about recent antibiotics/dental work). She has been taking ibuprofen most days for joint aches over the last month (reveal only if asked about pain medicines or over-the-counter drugs). NO travel, NO sick contacts she knows of, NO new foods. Occasional red, painful eye and migratory joint aches during flares (extraintestinal — only if asked).
PMH: ulcerative colitis (left-sided, diagnosed age 28); type 1 diabetes mellitus; iron-deficiency anemia in the past. MEDS (reveal individually when asked about medications): azathioprine 100 mg daily (immunosuppressant for her colitis); insulin glargine + lispro; ibuprofen most days for joint pain; sertraline for anxiety; a combined estrogen-progestin oral contraceptive pill. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: never smoked; rare alcohol; works as a teacher; mother has Crohn's disease (if asked about family). ROS: as above — also notes her blood sugars have been harder to control with the poor intake and stress.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, ill-appearing, mild distress, dry-looking.
- Vitals: BP 104/64 (orthostatic drop to 88/58 with a rise to 122 on standing if orthostatics checked), HR 108, RR 18, 38.1°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Mucous membranes: dry; skin turgor reduced (volume depletion).
- Abdomen: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, mild distension, NO rebound or rigidity and bowel sounds present at baseline (rebound/rigidity or a silent, tympanitic, distended abdomen would signal toxic megacolon/perforation); mildly tender to deep palpation.
- Possible aphthous mouth ulcers, a mildly injected painful eye, or tender joints if those areas are specifically examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent clindamycin/dental abscess, the regular ibuprofen use, the azathioprine immunosuppression, the OCP, the family history, or the extraintestinal eye/joint symptoms unless asked the relevant question.
- Reveal exam findings (orthostatic vitals, dry membranes, abdominal findings) only when that maneuver is actually performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 9.2 g/dL (microcytic anemia, MCV 78), WBC 14.8 with left shift, Plt 510 (reactive thrombocytosis). Consistent with a severe inflammatory flare and chronic GI blood loss.
labs.Chemistry: Na 134, K 2.9 (HYPOKALEMIA from diarrheal potassium loss), Cl 110, HCO3 16 → anion gap = 134 − (110 + 16) = 8 (NORMAL/low anion gap), i.e. a non-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis from lower-GI bicarbonate and potassium losses. Cr 1.4 (baseline 0.7) = AKI, BUN 32 — prerenal from volume depletion. Albumin 2.6 (low, protein-losing severe colitis). Glucose 224 (poorly controlled in the setting of illness).
labs.Inflammatory: CRP 96 mg/L and ESR 70 mm/hr markedly elevated; fecal calprotectin markedly elevated — active intestinal inflammation.
labs.StoolStudies: Stool bacterial cultures NEGATIVE; C. difficile GDH/toxin EIA and PCR NEGATIVE (no clostridioides infection despite the recent clindamycin); ova/parasites NEGATIVE. Stool CMV PCR not done on stool — addressed on biopsy below.
labs.Endoscopy: Flexible sigmoidoscopy (limited, gentle — full colonoscopy deferred in a severe flare to avoid perforation): CONTINUOUS, circumferential, friable mucosa with ulceration beginning at the rectum and extending proximally, no skip lesions or deep fissuring; biopsies show crypt distortion/branching and basal plasmacytosis (CHRONICITY of UC) with NO viral inclusion bodies and NEGATIVE CMV immunostain (excludes superimposed CMV colitis).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Flattened/inverted T waves with prominent U waves, mild ST depression, and a long QU interval — the electrocardiographic signature of hypokalemia (K 2.9) from her diarrheal potassium losses.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: flattened T waves with prominent U waves and a long QU interval consistent with hypokalemia. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing flattened T waves, prominent U waves, and a long QU interval of hypokalemia
searchTerms: flattened T wave prominent U wave long QU
ekg.clinicalVignette: A patient with a severe colitis flare, profuse diarrhea, and a serum potassium of 2.9 has a tracing performed for palpitations and lightheadedness.

cxr.label: Abdominal X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Non-dilated colon (transverse colon caliber well under 6 cm) with no thumbprinting, no free intraperitoneal air, and a normal small-bowel gas pattern — NO toxic megacolon or perforation at this time.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC abdominal film that is normal/non-dilated with no free air.
cxr.imageDescription: Upright abdominal radiograph, non-dilated colon, no free air.
cxr.clinicalVignette: A patient with a severe colitis flare and abdominal distension is imaged to screen for toxic megacolon and perforation.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical in this immunosuppressed patient with known UC, recent antibiotics, and regular NSAID use, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis). Name THREE infectious/drug-related mimics or superinfections you must exclude before escalating immunosuppression.
steps.rubric: Acute severe ulcerative colitis flare (leading,6). C. difficile infection — must EXCLUDE first given recent clindamycin (CANT-MISS,4). CMV colitis superinfection — must exclude given azathioprine immunosuppression (CANT-MISS,3). Other infectious colitis incl. enteric bacterial pathogens (core,2). NSAID-induced/ischemic colitis given chronic ibuprofen (core,2). Crohn's disease (core,2). Names THREE mimics/superinfections to exclude before steroids — e.g. C. difficile, CMV, bacterial enteric infection (core,4). Names leading dx while prioritizing exclusion of infection/superinfection (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring on a routine UC flare and escalating immunosuppression without considering C. diff/CMV (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: acute SEVERE ulcerative colitis flare in an established UC patient. Before escalating immunosuppression you must exclude THREE infectious/drug-related mimics or superinfections: (1) Clostridioides difficile infection — she just finished clindamycin; (2) CMV colitis — she is immunosuppressed on azathioprine; (3) another enteric bacterial infection (e.g. Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella, E. coli). Also consider NSAID-induced/ischemic colitis given her daily ibuprofen, and Crohn's disease given the family history. The recent antibiotics, immunosuppression, and NSAID use make this a layered picture — commit to a UC flare only after the infectious/superinfection workup returns.

criticalActions.item: C. difficile infection — must exclude first given recent clindamycin
CMV colitis superinfection — must exclude given immunosuppression

coreActions.item: NSAID-induced / ischemic colitis given chronic ibuprofen
Names three mimics/superinfections to exclude before steroids
Names leading dx while prioritizing exclusion of infection/superinfection

penalties.item: Anchoring on a routine UC flare and escalating immunosuppression without considering C. diff/CMV

mustNotMiss: C. difficile infection — must exclude first given recent clindamycin
CMV colitis superinfection — must exclude given immunosuppression
acceptableAlternatives: NSAID-induced / ischemic colitis given chronic ibuprofen

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Stool studies incl. cultures AND C. difficile GDH/toxin/PCR to EXCLUDE infection given recent clindamycin (CANT-MISS,3). CBC (1). Chemistry/BMP — to capture hypokalemia, non-gap acidosis, and AKI (CANT-MISS,2). Inflammatory markers — CRP/ESR, albumin, fecal calprotectin (2). Cautious flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy incl. CMV immunostain — defer full colonoscopy in severe flare (3). Abdominal X-ray to screen for toxic megacolon/perforation (2). EKG given hypokalemia/palpitations (1). PENALTY: starting steroids/immunosuppression before excluding infection (-2 — addressed in management); full colonoscopy in a severe flare risking perforation (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: stool cultures + C. difficile GDH/toxin and PCR (exclude infection after clindamycin) + O&P; CBC; Chemistry/BMP (look for the hypokalemia, non-anion-gap acidosis, and AKI); CRP/ESR, albumin, fecal calprotectin; type & screen and iron studies for the anemia; cautious flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy INCLUDING CMV immunostain (defer full colonoscopy in a severe flare to avoid perforation); abdominal X-ray to screen for toxic megacolon/perforation; EKG given the palpitations and hypokalemia.

criticalActions.item: Stool studies incl. cultures and C. difficile GDH/toxin/PCR to exclude infection

coreActions.item: Inflammatory markers — CRP/ESR, albumin, fecal calprotectin
Cautious flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy incl. CMV immunostain
Abdominal X-ray to screen for toxic megacolon/perforation

penalties.item: Starting steroids/immunosuppression before excluding infection (addressed in management)
Full colonoscopy in a severe flare risking perforation

requiredTests: Stool studies incl. cultures and C. difficile GDH/toxin/PCR to exclude infection
contraindicatedTests: Full colonoscopy in a severe flare risking perforation
orderSensitiveActions: Stool studies incl. cultures and C. difficile GDH/toxin/PCR to exclude infection

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG, obtained for palpitations and lightheadedness. Describe your findings and tie them to her bloodwork.
steps.rubric: Findings = hypokalemia pattern: flattened/inverted T waves with PROMINENT U waves, mild ST depression, and a long QU interval, in a patient whose K is 2.9 from diarrheal losses. CREDIT: identifies prominent U waves / flattened T waves (6); names the electrolyte cause — hypokalemia from diarrheal potassium loss, NOT primary ischemia (5); systematic read and recognizes the arrhythmia risk warranting K (and Mg) repletion (4). PENALTY: mistaking the U waves / ST changes for primary ischemia, or calling the tracing normal and missing the hypokalemia (-3).
keyFindings: prominent U waves with flattened T waves
long QU interval from hypokalemia (K 2.9), not primary ischemia
criticalFindings: Hypokalemia pattern — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, and a long QU interval from diarrheal potassium loss (K 2.9).
commonMistakes: reading the U waves / ST depression as primary myocardial ischemia
calling the tracing normal and missing the hypokalemia
steps.idealAnswer: Flattened/inverted T waves with prominent U waves, mild ST depression, and a long QU interval — the classic electrocardiographic signature of HYPOKALEMIA, here driven by her potassium of 2.9 from heavy diarrheal losses (not primary ischemia). This carries a real arrhythmia risk and mandates urgent potassium (and magnesium) repletion with telemetry, which feeds directly into the management plan.

criticalActions: Hypokalemia pattern — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, and a long QU interval from diarrheal potassium loss (K 2.9).
coreActions: prominent U waves with flattened T waves
long QU interval from hypokalemia (K 2.9), not primary ischemia

penalties.label: reads the hypokalemia pattern as ischemia or calls the study normal

steps.label: Read X-ray
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's upright abdominal film. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = non-dilated colon (transverse colon < 6 cm), no thumbprinting, no free intraperitoneal air. CREDIT: assesses colonic caliber and reports it is non-dilated (5); explicitly screens for/excludes toxic megacolon AND perforation/free air (5). PENALTY: missing the purpose of the film or overlooking free air (-1).
keyFindings: no free intraperitoneal air — no toxic megacolon or perforation
criticalFindings: Non-dilated colon with no free air — no toxic megacolon or perforation at this time.
commonMistakes: missing the purpose of the film
steps.idealAnswer: Non-dilated colon (transverse colon caliber well under 6 cm) with no thumbprinting and no free intraperitoneal air — i.e. NO toxic megacolon and NO perforation at this time. This is the deliberate screen in a severe flare; serial films are needed if she clinically deteriorates.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: non-dilated colon with no free air — no toxic megacolon or perforation.

coreActions.item: assesses colonic caliber and reports it non-dilated
explicitly screens for/excludes toxic megacolon and perforation/free air

penalties.item: missing the purpose / overlooking free air

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Stool C. difficile testing is negative, biopsy CMV immunostain is negative, and sigmoidoscopy shows continuous mucosal inflammation from the rectum with chronic-injury features. What is your diagnosis, what discriminators clinched it, and what residual uncertainty remains? Name three mechanisms by which her recent medications could have worsened or confounded this picture.
steps.rubric: Acute SEVERE ulcerative colitis flare (6). Cites the discriminators that excluded the mimics — negative C. difficile toxin/PCR (despite clindamycin), negative CMV immunostain/inclusions on biopsy, continuous rectum-up inflammation with chronicity (crypt distortion, basal plasmacytosis), and elevated CRP/calprotectin (2). Names three medication mechanisms — clindamycin predisposing to C. difficile, azathioprine immunosuppression predisposing to CMV reactivation, and NSAIDs (ibuprofen) triggering/worsening the flare; acknowledges residual uncertainty (e.g. CMV can be patchy, repeat testing if steroid-refractory) (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute SEVERE ulcerative colitis flare. Discriminators that clinched it over the mimics: a NEGATIVE C. difficile toxin and PCR (so the recent clindamycin did not cause C. diff colitis), a NEGATIVE CMV immunostain with no viral inclusions on biopsy (excludes CMV superinfection in this immunosuppressed patient), and CONTINUOUS inflammation extending up from the rectum with chronic-injury features (crypt distortion/branching, basal plasmacytosis) plus markedly elevated CRP/calprotectin — a flare of her known UC, not a new infection. Three medication mechanisms that worsened/confounded the picture: (1) clindamycin disrupted her gut flora and predisposed to C. difficile (the reason it had to be excluded); (2) azathioprine immunosuppression predisposes to CMV reactivation/colitis (the reason CMV had to be excluded); (3) regular ibuprofen (NSAID) can trigger or worsen an IBD flare and contributed to her AKI. Residual uncertainty: CMV can be patchy on biopsy, so if she fails to respond to steroids by day 3–5 the CMV question (and a repeat sigmoidoscopy/serum CMV PCR) must be revisited.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and what must be true before she could be safely discharged?
steps.rubric: Per MGH IBD: admit; IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) first-line for acute severe UC (CANT-MISS,5); confirm infection/C. diff and CMV excluded before/at escalation (CANT-MISS,3); aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with POTASSIUM (and magnesium) repletion on telemetry for the hypokalemia/EKG changes (CANT-MISS,3); VTE prophylaxis — high thrombotic risk, compounded by her OCP which should be held (2); STOP the NSAID/ibuprofen and avoid antimotility/opioids (3); rescue therapy (infliximab or cyclosporine) if steroid-refractory by day 3–5 (2); early surgery consult for toxic megacolon/perforation/refractory disease (2); GI consult and glycemic management of her T1DM on steroids (1); SAFE-DISCHARGE criteria — only when off IV steroids on a tolerated oral taper, hemodynamically stable with corrected electrolytes/resolving AKI, tolerating oral intake, with a steroid-sparing maintenance plan, VTE/bone-health counseling, and close GI follow-up (2). PENALTY: antimotility/opioids precipitating toxic megacolon (-3); escalating immunosuppression without excluding infection/CMV (-2); discharging before electrolytes corrected and infection excluded (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Admit (this is acute SEVERE UC). IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) first-line — but only after stool C. difficile and biopsy CMV are excluded; if not yet back, send them now and do not delay resuscitation. Aggressive IV fluids with POTASSIUM and magnesium repletion on telemetry given her K of 2.9 and hypokalemic EKG changes; recheck the chemistry to confirm the non-anion-gap acidosis and AKI are correcting. VTE prophylaxis (UC carries a high thrombotic risk) and HOLD the estrogen-containing OCP. STOP the ibuprofen (NSAIDs worsen flares and her AKI) and AVOID antimotility agents/opioids (precipitate toxic megacolon). If steroid-refractory by day 3–5, rescue with infliximab or cyclosporine. Early surgery consult for toxic megacolon, perforation, or refractory disease; GI consult; manage her type 1 diabetes glycemia (steroids will raise glucose, insulin needs). SAFE DISCHARGE only when: infection/CMV definitively excluded, hemodynamically stable with corrected potassium/magnesium and a resolving AKI, transitioned off IV steroids onto a tolerated oral steroid taper, tolerating oral intake, established on a steroid-sparing maintenance/biologic plan, counseled on VTE risk and steroid bone-health, restarted on appropriate prophylaxis, and with close GI follow-up arranged.

criticalActions.item: IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) first-line for acute severe UC
Exclude infection / C. diff and CMV before escalating immunosuppression
IV fluids with potassium (and magnesium) repletion on telemetry for the hypokalemia/EKG changes

coreActions.item: VTE prophylaxis and hold the estrogen-containing OCP
Stop the NSAID/ibuprofen and avoid antimotility/opioids
Rescue therapy (infliximab/cyclosporine) if steroid-refractory by day 3–5
Early surgery consult for toxic megacolon/perforation/refractory
GI consult and glycemic management of T1DM on steroids
Safe-discharge criteria: off IV steroids on tolerated oral taper, electrolytes corrected and AKI resolving, tolerating intake, maintenance plan, VTE/bone counseling, close GI follow-up

penalties.item: Discharging before electrolytes corrected and infection excluded

physicalExamMappings.finding: Orthostatic vitals: BP drops to 88/58 with HR rising to 122 on standing — orthostatic hypotension from diarrheal volume depletion.
Abdomen: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, mild distension, NO rebound or rigidity (rebound/rigidity would signal toxic megacolon/perforation); bowel sounds present.
Mucous membranes dry; reduced skin turgor — volume depletion.
Abdomen: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, mild distension, NO rebound or rigidity; bowel sounds present at baseline (a silent, distended, tympanitic abdomen would signal toxic megacolon).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 32F with established ulcerative colitis on azathioprine, type 1 diabetes, recent clindamycin for a dental abscess, and chronic NSAID use presents with 10 days of severe bloody diarrhea, volume depletion, hypokalemia with a hypokalemic EKG, a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and an AKI. The diagnosis is an acute SEVERE ulcerative colitis flare, reached only after excluding the layered infectious/drug-related mimics — C. difficile (recent clindamycin), CMV colitis (immunosuppression), other enteric infection, and NSAID/ischemic colitis — and confirming continuous rectum-up inflammation with chronic-injury features on biopsy.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Orthostatic vitals: BP drops to 88/58 with HR rising to 122 on standing — orthostatic hypotension from diarrheal volume depletion.
Abdomen: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, mild distension, NO rebound or rigidity (rebound/rigidity would signal toxic megacolon/perforation); bowel sounds present.
Mucous membranes dry; reduced skin turgor — volume depletion.
Abdomen: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, mild distension, NO rebound or rigidity; bowel sounds present at baseline (a silent, distended, tympanitic abdomen would signal toxic megacolon).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals in a known-UC patient who is immunosuppressed and recently took antibiotics.
Elicit the focused history, drawing out the recent clindamycin, regular NSAID use, azathioprine, OCP, and family history only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted exam — orthostatic vitals, hydration status, and abdominal exam screening for toxic megacolon.
Build a differential led by acute severe UC flare while explicitly excluding C. difficile, CMV colitis, other enteric infection, and NSAID/ischemic colitis before escalating immunosuppression.
Order a focused workup; read the hypokalemic EKG and the abdominal film for megacolon/perforation; integrate the multi-system labs (anemia, hypokalemia, non-gap acidosis, AKI, low albumin).

Commit to an acute severe UC flare and manage with resuscitation, electrolyte repletion on telemetry, IV steroids after infection is excluded, VTE prophylaxis, stopping NSAIDs/OCP, rescue therapy and surgery if refractory, and clear safe-discharge criteria.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a routine UC flare and escalating immunosuppression without excluding C. diff/CMV
Reading the hypokalemic U-wave EKG as primary ischemia
Discharging before electrolytes are corrected and infection excluded
managementPearls: IV corticosteroids after C. diff and CMV are excluded
Resuscitate and replete potassium/magnesium on telemetry for the hypokalemic EKG
criticalWorkupItems: Stool studies incl. cultures and C. difficile GDH/toxin/PCR to exclude infection
criticalInterpretationItems: Hypokalemia pattern — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, and a long QU interval from diarrheal potassium loss (K 2.9).
Non-dilated colon with no free air — no toxic megacolon or perforation at this time.
criticalManagementItems: IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone) first-line for acute severe UC
Exclude infection / C. diff and CMV before escalating immunosuppression
IV fluids with potassium (and magnesium) repletion on telemetry for the hypokalemia/EKG changes
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Hypokalemia pattern — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, and a long QU interval from diarrheal potassium loss (K 2.9).
Non-dilated colon with no free air — no toxic megacolon or perforation at this time.

Non-dilated colon with no free air — no toxic megacolon or perforation at this time.
imageInterpretationPearls: Prominent U waves with flattened T waves and a long QU in a patient with profuse diarrhea are the EKG signature of hypokalemia — replete and monitor, don't chase ischemia.
On a severe-flare abdominal film the deliberate screen is for colonic dilation (toxic megacolon) and free air (perforation).
Integrate the EKG with the chemistry — the tracing is the patient's own potassium of 2.9 made visible.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's fear about a severe flare
Asks permission before sensitive questions (medications, substance use)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
